claim: The embryonic medial and lateral ganglionic eminences (MGE, LGE) are the principal sources of most neurons and glia for the basal ganglia.
source: The embryonic medial and lateral ganglionic eminences (MGE, LGE) are the principal sources of most neurons and glia for the basal ganglia.
evidence: This is a foundational statement about the developmental origin of basal ganglia components, supported by established scientific knowledge in the field.

claim: In primates, the MGE has a distinctive cytoarchitecture characterized by doublecortin enriched cellular nests (DENs).
source: In primates, the MGE has a distinctive cytoarchitecture characterized by doublecortin enriched cellular nests (DENs)
evidence: This claim is supported by citations to previous studies (references 30, 31) that have characterized this primate-specific feature.

claim: Using paired single-nucleus transcriptomics and chromatin accessibility profiling of the three GEs, distinct progenitor populations were identified.
source: Here, using paired single-nucleus transcriptomics and chromatin accessibility profiling of the three GEs, we identify distinct progenitor populations
evidence: This claim is supported by the experimental data generated in this study using multiome sequencing.

claim: Live imaging reveals a unipolar outer radial glia-like population (GE-oRG) that undergoes mitotic somal translocation.
source: Live imaging reveals a unipolar outer radial glia-like population (GE-oRG) that undergoes mitotic somal translocation.
evidence: This claim is supported by live imaging experiments conducted in the study, as described in the methods and results sections.

claim: Spatial transcriptomics identifies a distinct CRABP1+/ANGPT2+ domain within the MGE.
source: Spatial transcriptomics identifies a distinct CRABP1+/ANGPT2+ domain within the MGE.
evidence: This claim is supported by spatial transcriptomics data generated in the study using MERFISH technology.

claim: Integrated spatial and electron microscopy demonstrates a periphery-to-center gradient of differentiation in the MGE.
source: Integrated spatial and electron microscopy demonstrates a periphery-to-center gradient of differentiation in the MGE.
evidence: This claim is supported by combined spatial transcriptomics and electron microscopy data presented in the study.

claim: PCDH19 is a key regulator of DEN formation.
source: Leveraging DEN-forming MGE organoids derived from PCDH19 knockout human pluripotent stem cell lines, we identify the protocadherin, PCDH19, as a key regulator of DEN formation.
evidence: This claim is supported by experimental data from PCDH19 knockout organoids showing reduced DEN formation compared to controls.

claim: The basal ganglia comprise the striatum, globus pallidus, subthalamic nucleus and substantia nigra.
source: The basal ganglia, comprising the striatum, globus pallidus, subthalamic nucleus and substantia nigra, are subcortical regions essential for motor control (Rocha et al., 2023).
evidence: This is a definitional statement supported by citation to established literature.

claim: The lateral ganglionic eminence (LGE) serves as the developmental origin of the striatum and is the main source of striatal projection neurons, predominantly medium spiny neurons (MSNs), as well as olfactory bulb interneurons.
source: During development, the lateral ganglionic eminence (LGE) serves as the developmental origin of the striatum acting and is the main source of striatal projection neurons, predominantly medium spiny neurons (MSNs), as well as olfactory bulb interneurons (de Carlos et al., 1996; Hamasaki et al., 2003; Stenman et al., 2003; Wichterle et al., 1999).
evidence: This claim is supported by multiple citations to previous studies establishing the developmental origin of striatal neurons.

claim: The medial ganglionic eminence (MGE) produces GABAergic interneurons destined for the striatum, globus pallidus and cortex, as well as projection neurons for the globus pallidus and other brain regions.
source: The medial ganglionic eminence (MGE) produces GABAergic interneurons destined for the striatum, globus pallidus and cortex, as well as projection neurons for the globus pallidus and other brain regions (Letinic et al., 2002; Sussel et al., 1999; Nunnelly et al., 2022; Ma et al., 2013; Anderson et al., 1997; Wamsley & Fishell, 2017; Rubenstein et al., 2013).
evidence: This claim is supported by multiple citations to previous studies characterizing MGE-derived cell types.

claim: The caudal ganglionic eminence (CGE) produces mainly cortical interneurons and may contribute to other forebrain structures.
source: A third GE region, the caudal ganglionic eminence (CGE) produces mainly cortical interneurons and may contribute to other forebrain structures (Letinic et al., 2002; Sussel et al., 1999; Nery et al., 2002; Prayer et al., 2006; Flames & Marin, 2005; Sorrells et al., 2019).
evidence: This claim is supported by multiple citations to previous studies characterizing CGE-derived cell types.

claim: The three GE domains are distinguished by characteristic gene signatures: NKX2-1 marks the MGE, NR2F2 and SP8 characterize the LGE and CGE, and DLX family genes are differentially expressed across all GE subregions.
source: The three GE domains are distinguished by characteristic gene signatures: NKX2-1 marks the MGE, NR2F2 and SP8 characterize the LGE and CGE, and DLX family genes, that are core regulators of GABAergic development (Lim et al., 2018; Rubenstein et al., 2024), are differentially expressed across all GE subregions.
evidence: This claim is supported by citations to previous studies and represents established knowledge about GE molecular markers.

claim: The subthalamic nucleus and substantia nigra are not derived from the ganglionic eminences.
source: The subthalamic nucleus and substantia nigra are not derived from the ganglionic eminences (Bokulic et al., 2022; Huang, 2015) and are therefore not considered in this study.
evidence: This claim is supported by citations to previous studies establishing the developmental origins of these structures.

claim: Recent single-cell transcriptomic studies have identified regionally distinct progenitor populations in the GEs.
source: Recent single-cell transcriptomic studies have broadened our understanding of GE cell type diversity across species (Shi et al., 2021; Lee et al., 2022; Turrero Garcia & Harwell, 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2022; Keefe et al., 2023; Cameron et al., 2023; Corrigan et al., 2024; Schmitz et al., 2022), identifying regionally distinct progenitor populations and revealing conserved developmental programs.
evidence: This claim is supported by multiple citations to recent single-cell transcriptomic studies.

claim: Primate GEs contain progenitor populations not found in rodents, including putative outer radial glia-like (oRG-like) cells and expanded intermediate progenitor (IPC) populations.
source: However, comparative analyses also suggest that primate GEs contain progenitor populations not found in rodents, including putative outer radial glia-like (oRG-like) cells and expanded intermediate progenitor (IPC) populations (Shi et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2022; Hansen et al., 2013; Paredes et al., 2022).
evidence: This claim is supported by citations to comparative studies between primate and rodent GE development.

claim: The mammalian GEs exhibit a unique densely packed cellular organization that differs markedly from the layered architecture of the cortex.
source: The mammalian GEs exhibit a unique densely packed cellular organization that differs markedly from the layered architecture of the cortex (Hansen et al., 2013; Paredes et al., 2022; Molnar et al., 2019; Casalia et al., 2021).
evidence: This claim is supported by citations to previous studies characterizing GE architecture.

claim: Primate GEs have an expanded subventricular zone (SVZ) enriched with IPCs, features that distinguish primate GEs from their rodent counterparts.
source: This dense organization is accompanied by an expanded subventricular zone (SVZ) enriched with IPCs, features that distinguish primate GEs from their rodent counterparts (Turrero Garcia & Harwell, 2017; Hansen et al., 2013; Paredes et al., 2022).
evidence: This claim is supported by citations to comparative studies between primate and rodent GE development.

claim: In humans and other large-brain primates, but not mice, the MGE exhibits a unique cytoarchitecture with vimentin/nestin-expressing cells encircling DCX⁺ cell–enriched nests (DENs).
source: In humans and other large-brain primates, but not mice, the MGE, but not LGE or CGE, exhibits a unique cytoarchitecture, with vimentin/nestin-expressing cells encircling DCX⁺ cell–enriched nests (DENs) (Hansen et al., 2013; Paredes et al., 2022).
evidence: This claim is supported by citations to previous studies characterizing primate-specific MGE architecture.

claim: The putative primate-specific MGE organization may contribute to the expanded generation of interneurons in large-brained primates.
source: This putative primate-specific MGE organization, may contribute to the expanded generation of interneurons in large-brained primates (Hansen et al., 2013; Paredes et al., 2022).
evidence: This is a speculative claim based on inference from the observed primate-specific architecture, supported by citations.

claim: The differential expression of the protocadherin PCDH19 in neuroblasts and PCDH10 in surrounding nestin+ progenitor clusters has suggested a possible role for protocadherins in DEN formation.
source: The differential expression of the protocadherin PCDH19 in neuroblasts and PCDH10 in surrounding nestin+ progenitor clusters has suggested a possible role for prorocadherins in DEN formation (Hansen et al., 2013).
evidence: This claim is supported by citation to a previous study that observed differential protocadherin expression.

claim: 20,319 high-quality nuclei were acquired for downstream analysis from the multiome sequencing.
source: Leveraging the 10x Genomics single-nucleus multiome platform, we generated paired RNA-seq and ATAC-seq data, from which 20,319 high-quality nuclei were acquired for downstream analysis.
evidence: This is a quantitative result from the experimental data processing.

claim: At the single-nucleus level, median values of 1,931 genes, 3,666 transcripts, and 8,980 ATAC peak fragments were observed.
source: At the single-nucleus level, we observed median values of 1,931 genes, 3,666 transcripts, and 8,980 ATAC peak fragments.
evidence: This is a quantitative result from the experimental data quality control.

claim: 31 clusters with molecularly distinct cell types were obtained, encompassing progenitors, neurons, and glia with clear segregation of LGE-, MGE-, and CGE-derived lineages.
source: Drawing on established annotations (Shi et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2022; Schmitz et al., 2022; Hansen et al., 2013; Yu et al., 2021; Ma et al., 2021), we obtained 31 clusters with molecularly distinct cell types encompassing progenitors, neurons, and glia and clear segregation of LGE-, MGE-, and CGE-derived lineages (Fig. 1A-D).
evidence: This claim is supported by the clustering analysis of the multiome data, with reference to Figure 1A-D.

claim: The progenitor compartment comprised clusters 0, 2, 5, 9, 11, 16, and 29, characterized by expression of one or more progenitor markers (SOX2, VIM, ASCL1, DLX family).
source: The progenitor compartment comprised clusters 0, 2, 5, 9, 11, 16, and 29, characterized by expression of one or more progenitor markers (SOX2, VIM, ASCL1, DLX family).
evidence: This claim is supported by the clustering analysis and marker gene expression patterns in the data.

claim: In the LGE-lineage, multiple medium spiny neuron (MSN) lineages were identified, including D1 MSNs (PDYN⁺ and TSHZ1⁺ lineages), D2 MSNs, and FOXP1/ISL1/NPY1R⁺ subpopulations.
source: In the LGE-lineage, we identified multiple medium spiny neuron (MSN) lineages, including D1 MSNs (PDYN⁺ and TSHZ1⁺ lineages), D2 MSNs, and FOXP1/ISL1/NPY1R⁺ subpopulations.
evidence: This claim is supported by the clustering analysis and marker gene expression patterns in the LGE-derived lineages.

claim: The MGE contained diverse interneuron subtypes, including SST⁺ and CRABP1⁺ populations.
source: The MGE contained diverse interneuron subtypes, including SST⁺ and CRABP1⁺ populations
evidence: This claim is supported by the clustering analysis and marker gene expression patterns in the MGE-derived lineages.

claim: The CGE contributed NR2F2/PROX1⁺ interneurons.
source: while the CGE contributed NR2F2/PROX1⁺ interneurons.
evidence: This claim is supported by the clustering analysis and marker gene expression patterns in the CGE-derived lineages.

claim: Subtype markers such as PV (PVALB) and VIP were not yet robustly detected at the developmental stages examined.
source: Subtype markers such as PV (PVALB), which emerges later in development (Abraham et al., 2023), and VIP were not yet robustly detected.
evidence: This claim is supported by the analysis of marker gene expression and citation to a study on PV developmental timing.

claim: Cholinergic (ChAT+) neurons were identified in the dataset.
source: We also identified cholinergic (ChAT+) neurons
evidence: This claim is supported by the clustering analysis and marker gene expression patterns.

claim: Ventromedial forebrain (VMF)-derived GBX1/GABRA1⁺ and ZIC1/ZIC2⁺ neuronal populations were identified.
source: as well as ventromedial forebrain (VMF)-derived GBX1/GABRA1⁺, ZIC1/ZIC2⁺ neuronal populations.
evidence: This claim is supported by the clustering analysis and marker gene expression patterns.

claim: Glial classes including astrocytes, oligodendrocyte precursor cells (OPCs), and microglia were present in the dataset.
source: In addition to neuronal lineages, glial classes were also present, including astrocytes, oligodendrocyte precursor cells (OPCs), and microglia.
evidence: This claim is supported by the clustering analysis and marker gene expression patterns.

claim: Distinct patterns of open chromatin shape both cellular identity and developmental potential.
source: Distinct patterns of open chromatin shape both cellular identity and developmental potential (Heintzman et al., 2009; Song et al., 2011; Junion et al., 2012; Todd et al., 2025; Cardamone et al., 2025).
evidence: This is a general principle supported by multiple citations to previous studies on chromatin accessibility.

claim: Differentially accessible regions (DARs) revealed distinct regulatory programs across progenitor states and lineage progression.
source: DARs revealed distinct regulatory programs across progenitor states and lineage progression (Fig. 1D, Supplemental Fig. 1, 2).
evidence: This claim is supported by the ATAC-seq analysis presented in the figures.

claim: Progenitor-wide DARs highlighted biological processes related to stem cell maintenance, proliferation, glial fate, and Notch signaling.
source: Progenitor-wide DARs (clusters 0, 2, 5, 9, 11, 16, 29) highlighted biological processes related to stem cell maintenance, proliferation, glial fate, and Notch signaling (Supplemental Fig. 2A, F, Data S1).
evidence: This claim is supported by the functional enrichment analysis of DARs in progenitor populations.

claim: As progenitor lineages progressed, they acquired DARs associated with GABAergic differentiation and olfactory bulb development.
source: As the progenitor lineages progressed (Fig. 1A, Supplemental Fig. 2A), they acquired DARs associated with GABAergic differentiation and olfactory bulb development (Supplemental Fig. 2F, Data S1), indicating emerging lineage competence.
evidence: This claim is supported by the functional enrichment analysis of DARs along developmental trajectories.

claim: MGE-derived lineage exhibited accessibility near genes governing GABAergic interneuron differentiation, migration, and maturation.
source: Lineage-specific DARs further delineated GABAergic regulatory programs: MGE-derived lineage (clusters 4, 6, 20, 23 in Fig. 1A) exhibited accessibility near genes governing GABAergic interneuron differentiation, migration, and maturation (Supplemental Fig. 2B, D, Data S1).
evidence: This claim is supported by the functional enrichment analysis of lineage-specific DARs.

claim: DARs in the SST+ interneurons were highly enriched for proximity to genes characteristic of cortical interneuron programs.
source: For example, DARs in the SST+ interneurons were highly enriched for proximity to genes related to characteristic of cortical interneuron programs.
evidence: This claim is supported by the functional enrichment analysis of DARs in SST+ interneurons.

claim: LGE-derived lineages showed enrichment for chromatin regions associated with olfactory bulb interneuron development, habituation, and dopaminergic pathways.
source: LGE-derived lineages, in turn, showed enrichment for chromatin regions associated with olfactory bulb interneuron development, habituation, and dopaminergic pathways, implicating their roles in sensory and reward-related processes (Supplemental Fig. 2C, E, Data S1).
evidence: This claim is supported by the functional enrichment analysis of lineage-specific DARs.

claim: Using SCENIC+, 555 eRegulons were identified, with 389 transcriptional activators and 166 repressors, targeting 45,013 regions and 6,048 genes.
source: Using this approach, we identified 555 eRegulons, with 389 transcriptional activators and 166 repressors, targeting 45,013 regions and 6,048 genes (Fig. 1E, F).
evidence: This is a quantitative result from the SCENIC+ gene regulatory network analysis.

claim: Activator gene regulatory network (GRNs) analysis recovered known master regulators of GE progenitors (SOX2, HES5, ASCL1, and DLX family genes) and regional TFs.
source: Activator gene regulatory network (GRNs) analysis recovered known master regulators of GE progenitors (SOX2, HES5, ASCL1, and DLX family genes) and regional TFs (Fig. 1E): MGE: NKX2-1, LHX6, LGE: ISL1, EBF1, MEIS2, SP8 and CGE: ZBTB16, SP8.
evidence: This claim is supported by the SCENIC+ analysis identifying known regulators, validated by existing knowledge.

claim: The MGE-CRABP1+ lineage showed the strongest enrichment of the NKX2-1–GRN among MGE-derived populations.
source: Notably, the MGE-CRABP1+ lineage showed the strongest enrichment of the NKX2-1–GRN among MGE-derived populations, suggesting a potential striatal-biased fate, as NKX2-1 expression is maintained in striatal interneurons but downregulated in cortical interneurons (Marin et al., 2000).
evidence: This claim is supported by the SCENIC+ analysis and inference based on known NKX2-1 expression patterns cited from previous work.

claim: The analysis uncovered less well characterized activator programs including PURG in the MGE-CRABP1+ lineage and ZFHX3 in the LGE MSN lineage.
source: Our analysis also uncovered activator programs that are less well characterized in this context, including PURG in the MGE-CRABP1+ lineage and ZFHX3 in the LGE MSN lineage (notably the D1 MSN PDYN subtype).
evidence: This claim is supported by the SCENIC+ analysis identifying novel regulators in specific lineages.

claim: Repressor-mediated GRNs acted more broadly across diverse cell types compared to activators.
source: In contrast to activators, which form highly cell-type specific GRNs, repressor-mediated GRNs acted more broadly across diverse cell types (Fig. 1F).
evidence: This claim is supported by the SCENIC+ analysis comparing activator and repressor GRN patterns.

claim: The widespread activity of repressor GRNs likely reflects their essential role in safeguarding cell identity by continuously suppressing alternative fate programs.
source: This widespread activity likely reflects their essential role in safeguarding cell identity by continuously suppressing alternative fate programs, thereby preserving lineage fidelity during neural development.
evidence: This is an inference based on the observed pattern of repressor GRN activity and general knowledge of repressor function.

claim: LGE and MGE lineages showed enrichment across multiple neuropsychiatric traits.
source: LGE and MGE lineages showed enrichment across multiple neuropsychiatric traits, highlighting lineage-specific vulnerability.
evidence: This claim is supported by the SCAVENGE analysis mapping GWAS variants to cell types.

claim: LGE-derived MSNs are associated with ADHD (D1 TSHZ1⁺).
source: LGE-derived MSNs are associated with ADHD (D1 TSHZ1⁺, Supplemental Fig. 3A, G), pointing to a striatal developmental association and potential links to dopaminergic pathways implicated in ADHD (Paloyelis et al., 2012; MacDonald et al., 2024; Sudre et al., 2023; Meijer et al., 2025)
evidence: This claim is supported by the SCAVENGE analysis and citations to studies linking striatal dopaminergic pathways to ADHD.

claim: Broad MSN enrichment is associated with MDD.
source: and MDD (broad MSN enrichment, Supplemental Fig. 3D, G).
evidence: This claim is supported by the SCAVENGE analysis showing MSN enrichment for MDD-associated variants.

claim: MGE-derived SST⁺ interneurons are strongly linked to SCZ.
source: MGE-derived SST⁺ interneurons are strongly linked to SCZ (Supplemental Fig. 3 F, G) aligning with their established roles in cortical inhibition central to schizophrenia pathogenesis (Sawada et al., 2024; Kos et al., 2018; Kiss et al., 2026; Patton et al., 2025; Dienel et al., 2023; McCutcheon et al., 2019).
evidence: This claim is supported by the SCAVENGE analysis and multiple citations to studies linking SST+ interneurons to schizophrenia.

claim: A VMF-derived GBX1⁺/GABRA1⁺ population, likely globus pallidus–destined, associates with MDD.
source: Additionally, a VMF-derived GBX1⁺/GABRA1⁺ population, likely globus pallidus–destined (Schmitz et al., 2022; Saunders et al., 2018), also associates with MDD (Supplemental Fig. 3D, G), consistent with accumulating evidence in the literature (Kim et al., 2019; Lacerda et al., 2003).
evidence: This claim is supported by the SCAVENGE analysis and citations to studies linking globus pallidus to MDD.

claim: Six major progenitor clusters were identified: RG subtypes or states (HES5⁺/VIM⁺/SOX2⁺/PDGFRB⁺), IPCs (ASCL1⁺/DLX1⁺/DLX2⁺), neuroblast populations (DCX⁺/DLX5⁺/DLX6+), cycling clusters (MKI67⁺/TOP2A⁺), oligodendrocyte precursor (OPC; OLIG2) and astrocyte progenitor (APC; ALDH1L1) clusters.
source: We identified six major clusters: RG subtypes or states (HES5⁺/VIM⁺/SOX2⁺/PDGFRB⁺), IPCs (ASCL1⁺/DLX1⁺/DLX2⁺), neuroblast populations (DCX⁺/DLX5⁺/DLX6+), where neuroblasts refer to IPC daughter cells strictly committed to a single neuron fate, along with cycling clusters (MKI67⁺/TOP2A⁺), oligodendrocyte precursor (OPC; OLIG2) and astrocyte progenitor (APC; ALDH1L1) clusters (Fig. 2A, B, Supplemental Fig. 4A, B, 5).
evidence: This claim is supported by the subclustering analysis of progenitor populations with marker gene expression.

claim: RG displayed transcriptionally distinct, regional biased subtypes across the LGE, MGE, and CGE.
source: RG displayed transcriptionally distinct, regional biased subtypes across the LGE, MGE, and CGE (Fig. 2C, Supplemental Fig. 5B).
evidence: This claim is supported by the analysis of RG marker gene expression patterns across regions.

claim: All RG expressed canonical markers (VIM, SOX2, PDGFRB), reflecting a shared progenitor identity.
source: All RG expressed canonical markers (VIM, SOX2, PDGFRB), reflecting a shared progenitor identity.
evidence: This claim is supported by the marker gene expression analysis of RG populations.

claim: Pan-RG markers (ATP1A2, PTPRZ1, TNC) showed broad expression across progenitor zones in the MGE and LGE, with RG in the MGE notably enriched around DENs.
source: Pan-RG markers (ATP1A2, PTPRZ1, TNC) showed broad expression across progenitor zones in the MGE and LGE, with RG in the MGE notably enriched around DENs (Supplemental Fig. 5, 6A).
evidence: This claim is supported by marker gene expression analysis and spatial validation.

claim: Regional specification was marked by NKX2-1 and SOX6 in MGE RG, NR2F2 in LGE/CGE RG, and PAX6 in the dorsal LGE and CGE.
source: Regional specification was marked by NKX2-1 and SOX6 in MGE RG (Supplemental Fig. 5B), NR2F2 in LGE/CGE RG, and PAX6 in the dorsal LGE and CGE (Supplemental Fig. 5B), reflecting early molecular diversification among RG populations.
evidence: This claim is supported by the analysis of regional marker gene expression in RG populations.

claim: Additional regionally enriched genes including RIC3 and MBIP in MGE RG and NTRK2 and GLI3 in L/CGE RG contributed to molecular separation across regional domains.
source: Consistent with previous studies (Shi et al., 2021), additional regionally enriched genes—including RIC3 and MBIP in MGE RG and NTRK2 and GLI3 in L/CGE RG—further contributed to the molecular separation across regional domains (Supplemental Fig. 5B).
evidence: This claim is supported by the marker gene analysis and citation to a previous study.

claim: Novel regional specific markers were identified, including PTPRQ in MGE RG and PRR16 in LGE RG.
source: We also identified novel regional specific markers (Fig. 2C), including PTPRQ in MGE RG (Fig. 2C, D) and PRR16 in LGE RG (Fig. 2C, E), which were validated by RNAscope.
evidence: This claim is supported by the marker gene analysis and validation by RNAscope.

claim: IPCs formed an early-to-late differentiation axis across regions with early IPCs expressing relatively higher SOX2, EGFR, HES5, and VIM, and late IPCs displaying reduced levels of these genes and increased levels of DLX1, DLX2, ASCL1, DLL1, and DLL3.
source: In our progenitor-enriched multiome dataset, IPCs formed an early-to-late differentiation axis across regions: early IPCs expressed relatively higher SOX2, EGFR, HES5, and VIM, consistent with residual RG-like features, whereas late IPCs displayed reduced levels of these genes and increased levels of DLX1, DLX2, ASCL1, DLL1, and DLL3.
evidence: This claim is supported by the analysis of gene expression patterns along the IPC differentiation trajectory.

claim: A progressive shift in DLX family expression was observed, with DLX1/2 defining late IPCs and early NBs and DLX5/6 marking the transition to DCX⁺ neuroblasts.
source: Consistent with the known sequential activation of DLX genes (Liu et al., 1997; Eisenstat et al., 1999), we observed a progressive shift in DLX family expression, with DLX1/2 defining late IPCs and early NBs and DLX5/6 marking the transition to DCX⁺ neuroblasts (Fig. 2B, Supplemental Fig. 5A, 6A).
evidence: This claim is supported by the gene expression analysis and citations to previous studies on DLX gene activation.

claim: Cell-cycle analysis showed that RGs exhibited substantial G1-phase enrichment, while IPCs were predominantly in S or G2/M phases.
source: Cell-cycle analysis showed that RGs exhibited substantial G1-phase enrichment, while IPCs were predominantly in S or G2/M phases (Supplemental Fig. 4E, F, 6B), consistent with their role as transit-amplifying progenitors.
evidence: This claim is supported by the cell cycle analysis of progenitor populations.

claim: A subset of MGE early IPCs exhibited OLIG2 and high EGFR expression, resembling cortical tri-potent IPCs.
source: Interestingly, a subset of MGE early IPCs exhibited OLIG2 and high EGFR expression, resembling cortical tri-potent IPCs that can produce OPCs, interneurons, and astrocytes (Wang et al., 2025).
evidence: This claim is supported by the gene expression analysis and comparison to cortical tri-potent IPCs described in a cited study.

claim: Immunostaining confirmed the presence of EGFR+/OLIG2+ progenitors, which were concentrated in the iSVZ and oSVZ.
source: Immunostaining confirmed the presence of EGFR+/OLIG2+ progenitors, which were concentrated in the iSVZ and oSVZ (Supplemental Fig. 6E)
evidence: This claim is supported by immunostaining validation of the EGFR+/OLIG2+ population.

claim: MGE neuroblasts segregated into NKX2-1-high (Neuroblast1–3, BEST3+/SFTA3+) and NKX2-1-low populations (LHX6+ Neuroblast8–9).
source: MGE neuroblasts segregated into NKX2-1-high (Neuroblast1–3, BEST3+/SFTA3+), and NKX2-1-low populations (LHX6+ Neuroblast8–9, Fig. 2C; Supplemental Fig. 5B).
evidence: This claim is supported by the clustering and marker gene expression analysis of MGE neuroblasts.

claim: Some neuroblasts were actively engaged in G2/M or S phase, most pronounced in MGE neuroblasts which are likely the highly proliferative neuroblasts previously identified in DENs.
source: Some neuroblasts were actively engaged in G2/M or S phase (Supplemental Fig. 4F, 6B), most pronounced in MGE neuroblasts which are likely the highly proliferative neuroblasts previously identified in DENs (Hansen et al., 2013).
evidence: This claim is supported by cell cycle analysis and inference based on previous characterization of DEN neuroblasts.

claim: Trajectory analysis using eRegulon revealed four divergent LGE trajectories: D1_MSN (PDYN), D1_MSN (TSHZ1), D2_MSN, and LGE_FOXP1/ISL1/NPY1R.
source: Trajectory analysis using eRegulon revealed four divergent LGE trajectories: D1_MSN (PDYN), D1_MSN (TSHZ1), D2_MSN, and LGE_FOXP1/ISL1/NPY1R (Fig. 2F, J, Supplemental Fig. 7A, F), corresponding to basal ganglia projection neurons.
evidence: This claim is supported by the trajectory analysis using Slingshot and eRegulon data.

claim: MGE progenitors diverged into two major lineages (SST⁺ and CRABP1⁺).
source: At the same time, MGE progenitors diverged into two major lineages (SST⁺ and CRABP1⁺; Fig. 2H, I, Supplemental Fig. 8B, G).
evidence: This claim is supported by the trajectory analysis of MGE lineages.

claim: NKX2-1 dynamics segregated MGE fates: CRABP1+ cells retained NKX2-1, suggesting a striatal fate, while SST+ cells downregulated NKX2-1, indicating a cortical fate.
source: NKX2-1 dynamics segregated MGE fates (Marin et al., 2000): CRABP1+ cells retained NKX2-1, suggesting a striatal fate, while SST+ cells downregulated NKX2-1, indicating a cortical fate (Supplemental Fig. 7C).
evidence: This claim is supported by the analysis of NKX2-1 expression along trajectories and citation to a study on NKX2-1 function.

claim: This bifurcation displayed a temporal difference: CRABP1⁺ interneurons were predominantly represented at GW14 samples, whereas SST⁺ cells were more abundant at GW20, suggesting that striatal interneurons are produced earlier than cortical interneurons.
source: Notably, this bifurcation also displayed a temporal difference: CRABP1⁺ interneurons were predominantly represented at GW14 samples, whereas SST⁺ cells were more abundant at GW20, suggesting that striatal interneurons are produced earlier than cortical interneurons (Supplemental Fig. 7D).
evidence: This claim is supported by the temporal analysis of cell type proportions and represents an inference about developmental timing.

claim: Gene signatures characteristic of PV neuroblasts and immature neurons were enriched within the CRABP1⁺ lineage.
source: Rather than identifying a distinct PV trajectory, we identified gene signatures characteristic of PV neuroblasts and immature neurons (Ma et al., 2021) enriched within the CRABP1⁺ lineage (Supplemental Fig. 7E)
evidence: This claim is supported by gene signature analysis and citation to a study defining PV signatures.

claim: HOPX was only expressed in a subpopulation of L/CGE RG, but not in MGE RG.
source: Interestingly, HOPX was only expressed in a subpopulation of L/CGE RG, but not in MGE RG (RG1, Supplemental Fig. 5B; Supplemental Fig. 10A, B), suggesting HOPX may be a regional oRG identity marker rather than a universal oRG marker.
evidence: This claim is supported by gene expression analysis and immunostaining validation.

claim: Across all GEs, dividing non-ventricular RG-like cells with a prominent basal process underwent MST consistent with oRG identity.
source: Across all GEs, dividing non-ventricular RG-like cells with a prominent basal process underwent MST consistent with oRG identity (Fig. 3B-D).
evidence: This claim is supported by live imaging observations of cell division behavior.

claim: In the MGE at GW13–14, MST divisions predominated compared to IPC divisions (63.5% vs 25.0%, p = 0.0419).
source: In the MGE at GW13–14, MST divisions predominated compared to IPC divisions (63.5% vs 25.0%, p = 0.0419) (Fig. 3C, D)
evidence: This is a quantitative result from live imaging analysis with statistical testing.

claim: In the CGE, IPC-like (32.5%, p = 0.0491) and oRG-like (58.5%, p = 0.046) divisions were significantly more frequent than RG-like interkinetic nuclear migration (INM events, 9.0%).
source: In the CGE, IPC-like (32.5%, p = 0.0491) and oRG-like (58.5%, p = 0.046) divisions were significantly more frequent than RG-like interkinetic nuclear migration (INM events, 9.0%).
evidence: This is a quantitative result from live imaging analysis with statistical testing.

claim: By GW17–19, cells undergoing IPC-like mitotic rounding prior to division increased in the MGE (63.0%, p = 0.0305) and LGE (45.0%, p = 0.0123), marking a shift toward differentiation.
source: By GW17–19, cells undergoing IPC-like mitotic rounding prior to division increased in the MGE (63.0%, p = 0.0305) and LGE (45.0%, p = 0.0123), marking a shift toward differentiation, whereas proportions remained stable in the CGE (44.3%, Fig. 3C, D).
evidence: This is a quantitative result from live imaging analysis with statistical testing and interpretation.

claim: MST translocation distances were longest in the CGE (65.2 µm) compared with the MGE (46.3 µm) and LGE (47.8 µm; p < 0.025).
source: MST translocation distances were longest in the CGE (65.2 µm) compared with the MGE (46.3 µm) and LGE (47.8 µm; p < 0.025) (Fig. 3G; Supplemental Fig. 10D-F)
evidence: This is a quantitative result from live imaging analysis with statistical testing.

claim: MST directionality was largely random.
source: MST directionality was largely random (Supplemental Fig. 10J), consistent with the non-radial orientation of GE fibers (Hansen et al., 2013; Paredes et al., 2022).
evidence: This claim is supported by quantitative analysis of MST directionality and citations to previous studies on GE fiber organization.

claim: Most MST divisions were orthogonal (60–90°) to the main process (71–95% across regions and ages).
source: Most MST divisions were orthogonal (60–90°) to the main process (71–95% across regions and ages) (Supplemental Fig. 10G-I), resembling cortical oRG behavior (LaMonica et al., 2013; Gertz et al., 2014).
evidence: This is a quantitative result from live imaging analysis with comparison to cortical oRG behavior from cited studies.

claim: Transcriptional divergence between vRGs and oRGs is less pronounced in the ganglionic eminences than in cortex.
source: Second, transcriptional divergence between vRGs and oRGs is less pronounced in the ganglionic eminences than in cortex (Pollen et al., 2015).
evidence: This claim is supported by comparison to cortical data and citation to a previous study.

claim: The human MGE increases in volume from approximately 115 mm³ at GW 18 to 158 mm³ at GW 22.
source: The human MGE increases in volume from approximately 115 mm³ at GW 18 to 158 mm³ at GW 22.
evidence: This is a quantitative measurement cited from a previous study (reference 30).

claim: Dividing GE-oRGs account for a substantial proportion of mitotic events.
source: Given that dividing GE-oRGs account for a substantial proportion of mitotic events, we propose they may contribute to progenitor zone expansion in the ganglionic eminences
evidence: This is an inference based on the quantitative data on division proportions from live imaging.

claim: Regional differences between cortical and GE RG are more pronounced at the epigenomic than the transcriptomic level.
source: Our comparison of cortical and ganglionic eminence RG revealed that regional differences are more pronounced at the epigenomic than the transcriptomic level.
evidence: This claim is supported by comparative analysis of transcriptomic and epigenomic data.

claim: Overlap of marker chromatin peaks between cortical and GE RG is limited (19.3% of marker peaks versus 50.2% of marker genes).
source: Overlap of marker chromatin peaks is limited (19.3% of marker peaks versus 50.2% of marker genes, Supplemental Fig. 9D, 8D)
evidence: This is a quantitative result from comparative analysis of marker peaks and genes.

claim: Only 53.7% of highly variable chromatin peaks are shared between cortical and GE RG, compared with 77.7% of highly variable genes.
source: Only 53.7% of highly variable chromatin peaks are shared between regions, compared with 77.7% of highly variable genes (Supplemental Fig. 9D, 8D).
evidence: This is a quantitative result from comparative analysis of variable features.

claim: Enhancers are inherently more cell-type specific than transcriptional signatures alone.
source: Indeed, enhancers are inherently more cell-type specific than transcriptional signatures alone (Heinz et al., 2015; Furlong & Levine, 2018).
evidence: This is a general principle supported by citations to previous studies.

claim: MGE-derived striatal interneurons such as TAC3+ cells and cortical GABAergic interneurons including CGE-derived populations are present at higher proportions in primates than in rodents.
source: MGE-derived striatal interneurons such as TAC3+ cells (Schmitz et al., 2022; Corrigan et al., 2024; Garma et al., 2024; Krienen et al., 2020) and cortical GABAergic interneurons including CGE-derived populations (Krienen et al., 2020; Fang et al., 2022; Marin, 2025) are present at higher proportions in primates than in rodents.
evidence: This claim is supported by multiple citations to comparative studies between primate and rodent interneuron populations.

claim: Neuronal amplification may be driven by three hierarchically organized progenitor tiers: (1) non-apical GE outer radial glia (GE-oRGs), (2) intermediate progenitor cells (IPCs) and (3) proliferative neuroblasts.
source: We propose that neuronal amplification may be driven by three hierarchically organized progenitor tiers: (1) non-apical GE outer radial glia (GE-oRGs), (2) intermediate progenitor cells (IPCs) and (3) proliferative neuroblasts.
evidence: This is a model proposed based on integration of multiple lines of evidence from the study.

claim: The broad distribution of ATP1A2+/TNC+/PTPRZ1+ RG throughout the oSVZ of the human LGE and MGE, especially encircling MGE DENs, combined with their high frequency of MST, suggests that GE-oRGs contribute substantially to progenitor amplification.
source: First, the broad distribution of ATP1A2+/TNC+/PTPRZ1+ RG throughout the oSVZ of the human LGE and MGE, especially encircling MGE DENs, combined with their high frequency of MST, suggests that GE-oRGs contribute substantially to progenitor amplification.
evidence: This is an inference based on spatial distribution data and live imaging observations.

claim: ASCL1⁺ IPCs form a dense inner SVZ layer in human tissue, a structure absent in the mouse.
source: Second, consistent with previous observations of expanded progenitor populations in the primate MGE (Shi et al., 2021), we find that ASCL1⁺ IPCs form a dense inner SVZ layer in human tissue, a structure absent in the mouse (Xu et al., 2014).
evidence: This claim is supported by spatial analysis and comparison to mouse data from a cited study.

claim: Ultrastructural and immunohistochemical analyses show that ASCL1⁺ IPCs separate adjacent MGE DENs, positioning them to efficiently supply newly generated neuroblasts to DENs.
source: Ultrastructural and immunohistochemical analyses further show that ASCL1⁺ IPCs separate adjacent MGE DENs, positioning them to efficiently supply newly generated neuroblasts to DENs.
evidence: This claim is supported by electron microscopy and immunostaining data.

claim: DENs contain DCX+/KI67+ proliferative neuroblasts that retain proliferative capacity despite expressing neuronal markers.
source: Third, DENs contain DCX+/KI67+ proliferative neuroblasts that retain proliferative capacity despite expressing neuronal markers (Hansen et al., 2013).
evidence: This claim is supported by immunostaining data and citation to a previous study.

claim: By GW23, CRABP1⁺/ANGPT2⁺ interneuron precursors consolidate into a well-defined territorial domain within the MGE.
source: Spatial transcriptomics revealed that by GW23 CRABP1⁺/ANGPT2⁺ interneuron precursors consolidate into a well-defined territorial domain within the MGE.
evidence: This claim is supported by spatial transcriptomics data.

claim: The CRABP1⁺/ANGPT2⁺ domain comprises postmitotic neurons and gives rise to two distinct migratory streams, one extending toward the LGE and cortex and another toward the striatum.
source: The CRABP1⁺/ANGPT2⁺ domain comprises postmitotic neurons and gives rise to two distinct migratory streams, one extending toward the LGE and cortex and another toward the striatum.
evidence: This claim is supported by spatial transcriptomics data and immunostaining validation.

claim: Persistent NKX2-1 expression could bias a subset of CRABP1+ cells toward striatal compartments via its downstream transcriptional network.
source: Persistent NKX2-1 expression, known to direct striatal interneuron migration (Nobrega-Pereira et al., 2008), could bias a subset of cells toward striatal compartments via its downstream transcriptional network.
evidence: This is an inference based on known NKX2-1 function cited from a previous study.

claim: ANGPT2 secretion by CRABP1+ cells may promote endothelial–neural crosstalk, providing metabolic, growth factor, and angiogenic signals to stabilize the niche and support maturation.
source: Concurrently, ANGPT2 secretion by these cells may promote endothelial–neural crosstalk (Crouch et al., 2022; Miners et al., 2023), providing metabolic, growth factor, and angiogenic signals, to stabilize the niche and support maturation.
evidence: This is a speculative claim based on known ANGPT2 function cited from previous studies.

claim: Serial EM reconstruction shows that RG processes form columns of interconnected fibers that surround and separate multiple stacked DENs.
source: Serial EM reconstruction shows that RG processes form columns of interconnected fibers that surround and separate multiple stacked DENs.
evidence: This claim is supported by electron microscopy data.

claim: DCX+/KI67+ neuroblasts within DENs have been proposed to serve as a major amplifying cell type in the MGE.
source: While radial glial processes provide the structural scaffold organizing DEN architecture, the DCX+/KI67+ neuroblasts within DENs have been proposed to serve as a major amplifying cell type in the MGE (Hansen et al., 2013).
evidence: This claim is supported by citation to a previous study that proposed this model.

claim: Highly proliferative GE-oRGs and IPCs that surround and separate individual DENs are additional drivers of neuronal amplification within the human MGE.
source: Our structural analysis and live imaging extend this model by demonstrating that highly proliferative GE-oRGs and IPCs that surround and separate individual DENs are additional drivers of neuronal amplification within the human MGE.
evidence: This is an extension of the previous model based on new data from this study.

claim: BMP-mediated signaling from RG to neuroblasts, and NOTCH-mediated signaling from IPC to neuroblasts, suggest that GE-oRGs and IPCs may actively sustain DEN growth by continually supplying newly generated neuroblasts.
source: Moreover, our analysis of cell-cell communication further supports this model. BMP-mediated signaling from RG to neuroblasts, and NOTCH-mediated signaling from IPC to neuroblasts, suggest that GE-oRGs and IPCs may actively sustain DEN growth by continually supplying newly generated neuroblasts.
evidence: This is an inference based on cell-cell communication analysis.

claim: Type 1 and 2 progenitor clusters and mature DEN-like structures have not been observed in organoids before this study.
source: However, to our knowledge type 1 and 2 progenitor clusters (Hansen et al., 2013; Paredes et al., 2022) and mature DEN-like structures have not been observed in organoids before our study.
evidence: This is a claim about novelty based on review of previous literature.

claim: Using MGE organoids derived from PCDH19 knockout (KO) PSCs, PCDH19 is required for DEN formation.
source: Using MGE organoids derived from PCDH19 knockout (KO) PSCs, we show that PCDH19 is required for DEN formation.
evidence: This claim is supported by experimental data comparing PCDH19 KO to control organoids.

claim: Loss-of-function mutations in PCDH19 cause Protocadherin-19 Clustering Epilepsy (PCE), a severe early-onset form of epilepsy.
source: Loss-of-function mutations in PCDH19 cause Protocadherin-19 Clustering Epilepsy (PCE), a severe early-onset form of epilepsy (Samanta, 2020; Kowkabi et al., 2024; Depienne et al., 2009; Marini et al., 2010; Dibbens et al., 2008; Jamal et al., 2010)
evidence: This is established knowledge supported by multiple citations to clinical studies.

claim: The prevailing pathogenic mechanism of PCE involves abnormal cell sorting driven by differential adhesion between mutant and wild-type cells.
source: whose prevailing pathogenic mechanism involves abnormal cell sorting driven by differential adhesion between mutant and wild-type cells.
evidence: This is established knowledge about the disease mechanism.

claim: Aberrant segregation in PCE has been demonstrated in both mouse models and human cortical organoids.
source: This aberrant segregation has been demonstrated in both mouse models (Pederick et al., 2018) and human cortical organoids (Tidball et al., 2023; Niu et al., 2024).
evidence: This claim is supported by citations to previous studies in mouse and organoid models.

claim: In mosaic cortical organoids containing a mixture of PCDH19-knockout and wild-type cells, the two populations spontaneously segregate into separate domains, a process termed cellular interference.
source: In mosaic cortical organoids containing a mixture of PCDH19-knockout and wild-type cells, the two populations spontaneously segregate into separate domains, a process termed cellular interference (Niu et al., 2024).
evidence: This claim is supported by citation to a previous study demonstrating cellular interference.

claim: Mosaicism is required for cellular segregation to occur in PCDH19 models.
source: Notably, mosaicism is required for this segregation to occur (Niu et al., 2024; Gecz & Thomas, 2020).
evidence: This claim is supported by citations to previous studies demonstrating the requirement for mosaicism.

claim: A differential protocadherin combinatorial code likely underlies DENs development, in which PCDH19-expressing neuroblasts segregate apart from PCDH10-expressing early progenitors.
source: Extending this framework to the developing MGE, we propose that a differential protocadherin combinatorial code likely underlies DENs development, in which PCDH19-expressing neuroblasts segregate apart from PCDH10-expressing early progenitors, thereby driving the self-organization of DENs.
evidence: This is a proposed model based on integration of PCDH19 KO data with previous observations of differential protocadherin expression.
